0.title: Admitted Patients (IPD)
0.text:
Admitted Patients (IPD)
Inpatient care (IPD) covers a patient’s whole stay — from admission to a bed,
through ward rounds and medications, to discharge. The admission record ties
all of it together.


  

Who does what
Operations staff and the Tenant Admin admit, transfer and discharge
patients. Doctors record ward rounds and inpatient medications.
Nurses and staff can view the stay and keep beds ready.

Admitting a patient
To admit a patient you choose a patient and a vacant bed, then record:

The admitting doctor
The reason for admission (primary diagnosis)
The expected length of stay (optional)
Payment details — any advance deposit, the payment type (self-pay,
cashless or corporate), and insurance provider and policy number if relevant

When you confirm, the bed is marked occupied and the patient is ready for
rounds and medications.


  
  

Safeguards on admission
A patient can only be admitted to a bed that’s vacant, and a patient who’s
already admitted can’t be admitted again.

Admission status



Status
Meaning




Active
The patient is currently in the hospital


Discharged
The patient has left; the bed is freed for cleaning


Transferred
The patient has been moved to a different bed



Ward rounds
Doctors record a round each time they review an admitted patient. A round
can capture:

Notes on the visit
Vitals — blood pressure, temperature, pulse, oxygen level (SpO₂) and
breathing rate
The assessment and the plan for the patient

Rounds build up a clear, time-ordered history of the stay that the whole
care team can see.
Inpatient medications
Unlike a single outpatient prescription, an admitted patient can have
medications added and revised throughout the stay. For each medicine the
doctor records the dosage, frequency, route (such as oral or IV), start and
(optional) end dates, instructions, and quantity, and whether it’s currently
active.
Transferring a bed
If a patient needs to move — say from a general ward to the ICU — operations
staff can transfer them to another vacant bed. The old bed is freed for
cleaning and the new bed becomes occupied, while the same admission (with all
its rounds and medications) continues uninterrupted.
Discharging a patient
When the patient is ready to leave, staff process the discharge. MedOps
records the discharge date, marks the admission discharged, and flags the
bed as reserved for cleaning so it’s prepared before the next patient.
How charges work during a stay

An advance deposit, payment type and insurance details are
captured at admission.
The room’s daily tariff (set per room type) determines the bed charges
for the stay. See
room pricing.
Any medicines dispensed by the pharmacy are billed by the pharmacy.

Related pages

Hospitals & Departments — set up
the beds you admit into
An Inpatient Admission — the full
walkthrough

8.title: Admitted Patients (IPD) > How charges work during a stay

10.title: Appointments
10.text:
Appointments
Appointments connect a patient with a doctor at a specific time. MedOps
checks each booking against the doctor’s real availability, so clashes are
prevented before they happen.


  

Who manages this
Operations staff and the Tenant Admin book and manage appointments.
Doctors see their own appointments in their queue but don’t book them.

Booking an appointment
When you book, you set:

Patient — the registered patient (this can’t be changed afterwards)
Doctor — optional, and must work at the patient’s hospital
Visit type — Outpatient (OP), Inpatient (IP), Emergency, Consultation,
or Follow-up
Date and time — must be in the future
Reason for visit — optional
Bed — required for an inpatient (IP) visit; must be a vacant bed

Booking on someone’s behalf
For an outpatient visit booked for a family member or dependant, you can add
beneficiary details: their name and age (required together), plus an
optional weight and relationship to the registered patient.
Appointment statuses
An appointment moves through these states:



Status
Meaning




Scheduled
Booked and awaiting the visit (the usual starting point)


Admitted
The starting point for an inpatient appointment


Completed
The visit (and any consultation) is finished


Cancelled
The appointment was cancelled


No-show
The patient didn’t arrive



How MedOps prevents clashes


  
  

You can’t create an invalid booking

No past bookings — appointments must be in the future.
Within working hours — a doctor can only be booked during the hours
they’ve set.
Not during leave or time-off — the doctor’s leave and blocked times
are off-limits.
No double-booking — slots already taken for that doctor on that day
are shown so they can’t be picked again.
Only free beds — an inpatient visit can only use a vacant bed.


When you choose a doctor and date, MedOps shows their upcoming leave and
blocked times, and which slots are already booked — so you can pick a free
time with confidence.
Rescheduling and updating
After booking, you can change:

The date and time (re-checked against the doctor’s availability)
The doctor (re-checked against the hospital and availability)
The visit type, reason, and beneficiary details
The status — for example, marking it completed, cancelled or
no-show

The patient on an appointment can’t be changed — book a new appointment if
it’s for someone else.
From appointment to consultation
When a doctor opens one of their appointments and starts a consultation,
the visit record and the clinical record are linked. Completing the
consultation also completes the appointment and generates the relevant bills.

Consultations
My Appointments & Availability

18.title: Activity Log
18.text:
Activity Log
The activity log is your organisation’s record of important actions — who did
what, when, and whether it succeeded. It’s there for accountability, for
answering “who changed this?”, and for peace of mind.


  

Who can see this
The activity log is available to the Tenant Admin, giving a single,
trustworthy view across the whole organisation.

What’s recorded
Each entry captures:

What happened — a short, readable summary of the action
Who did it — the person’s email and role
When — the date and time
Whether it succeeded or failed
What was affected — the kind of record involved (such as a patient,
appointment or bill)
A category to group it — for example, patients, appointments, clinical,
pharmacy, billing, administration, or sign-in

What gets logged
Actions that change something are recorded, including:

Registering and updating patients
Booking, updating and cancelling appointments
Clinical activity such as consultations and prescriptions
Pharmacy activity such as stock and invoices
Billing — creating, paying and cancelling bills
Administration — managing staff, hospitals, departments and beds
Sign-in attempts, including failed ones



  
  

Just looking doesn’t create noise
Simply viewing lists or opening records isn’t logged — only actions that
change data are, which keeps the log meaningful and easy to scan.

Finding what you need

Search across summaries, people and affected records
Filter by category to focus on one area (for example, billing)
Filter by date range to zoom in on a specific period
Entries are shown newest first

22.title: Activity Log > Finding what you need

23.title: Billing & Payments
23.text:
Billing & Payments
MedOps creates bills for you as care is delivered, so charges are never
missed. The front desk then records payments and keeps track of what’s still
outstanding.


  

Who manages this
The Tenant Admin has full oversight of billing. Operations staff
record payments and cancel bills as part of the front desk.

How bills are created
Bills are generated automatically:

A consultation fee bill is created when a doctor completes a
consultation (using the doctor’s set fee).
A medicine (prescription) bill is created for prescribed medicines.

Each bill shows the patient (with MRN), the hospital, the bill
type, the amount, and a description of what it’s for.


  
  
  

Pharmacy invoices
When the pharmacy dispenses medicines, it produces its own invoices in the
Pharmacy Portal, priced at the pharmacy’s rates
and with tax applied.

Bill status



Status
Meaning




Unpaid
The bill has been created but not yet paid


Paid
Payment has been received and recorded


Cancelled
The bill was cancelled (for example, created in error)



Recording a payment
When a patient pays, mark the bill as paid and record how it was
settled:

Cash
Bank transfer — with the transaction reference

MedOps stamps the time the payment was recorded.
Finding and managing bills

Browse and search bills by patient name, MRN or description.
Filter by hospital, bill type or payment status — for example, to see
everything that’s still unpaid.
Cancel a bill that was created in error.

Related pages

Consultations — where consultation bills come
from
Pharmacy Portal — pharmacy invoicing

25.title: Billing & Payments > How bills are created

28.title: Billing & Payments > Finding and managing bills

30.title: Consultations
30.text:
Consultations
A consultation is the record of a doctor seeing a patient. It captures
what happened during the visit and ties together the prescription and the
bill — so finishing a consultation takes care of the paperwork in one step.


  
  
  
  
  

Who uses this
Doctors record consultations for their own appointments. The Tenant
Admin can view consultations for oversight.

What a consultation captures

Clinical notes — your observations and findings
Follow-up date — optional, for when the patient should return
A prescription — optional, added as you complete the consultation
Status — either In progress or Completed

Each consultation is linked to the appointment and the patient, so it always
sits in the right place in the patient’s history.
Recording a consultation
Start the consultation from one of your appointments. It opens as
In progress.
Record your notes and update them as needed during the visit.
Add a prescription if the patient needs medicines — search the
catalogue and set the dosage, frequency and duration for each.
Set a follow-up date if appropriate.
Complete the consultation to finish up.

What happens when you complete a consultation


  

Three things happen together
The moment you complete a consultation, MedOps automatically:

Issues the prescription (if you added medicines), ready for the
pharmacy and for printing.
Creates the consultation bill using the doctor’s consultation fee, so
the front desk can collect payment.
Marks the appointment as completed.

These happen as a single action — there’s nothing extra to remember.

If the doctor doesn’t have a consultation fee set, no consultation bill is
created — everything else still works the same way.
Related pages

Prescriptions — what’s on the prescription and
how it reaches the pharmacy
Billing & Payments — collecting the consultation fee
An Outpatient Visit — the full walkthrough

34.title: Consultations > What happens when you complete a consultation

36.title: Dashboard
36.text:
Dashboard
When you sign in, you land on your home screen — a starting point
tailored to your role. It puts the work you do most within easy reach and
gives you an at-a-glance sense of what needs attention.
A view that fits your role
Different roles need different things, so the home screen adapts:



Role
What’s front and centre




Operations
Today’s appointments, patient registration, admissions and payments


Doctor
Your appointment queue and admitted patients


Nurse / Staff
Admitted patients and ward information


Pharmacy
Stock status, prescription lookup and billing


Tenant Admin
Setup, staff, billing and the activity log



At-a-glance numbers
From your lists you can quickly gauge the state of the day — for example:

How many appointments are scheduled, completed, cancelled or
marked no-show
Which bills are still unpaid
Which beds are vacant or occupied

Every list supports paging and filters, so you can narrow down to exactly
what you need.
Global search
A single search box helps you find things fast, wherever you are.


  
  

Search sees what your role sees
Search results are limited to the records your role is allowed to view, and
always stay within your own organisation.

Depending on your role, search can find:

Patients — by name, MRN, phone or email
Doctors — by name, specialisation or registration number
Appointments — by patient name, MRN, reason or beneficiary
Bills — by description, patient name or MRN
Hospitals & departments — by name, branch or city
Medicines — by name, code, strength or form



  
  
  

Two characters is enough
Start typing at least two characters and MedOps shows the closest matches
across the categories you can access.

38.title: Dashboard > A view that fits your role

41.title: Managing Doctors
41.text:
Managing Doctors
Doctors are added and managed by the Tenant Admin. Once a doctor is set
up and has accepted their invitation, they can manage their own availability
and start seeing patients.


  

Who manages this
The Tenant Admin adds and edits doctors. The front desk sees a simple
list of doctors (name, specialisation and department) when booking
appointments, without any private details.

Adding a doctor
When you add a doctor, you provide:

Personal details — full name, gender, date of birth, email and phone
Hospital — the hospital they work at
Department — optional, within that hospital
Credentials — medical council registration number, medical degree
(e.g. MBBS, MD), specialisation, and year of registration
Employment type — full-time, part-time, visiting, consultant or locum
Consultation fee — the fee charged per visit (optional; if left blank,
no consultation fee is billed)

When you save, MedOps emails the doctor a welcome message with a link to set
their own password and sign in.


  
  

Invitations can be re-sent
If a doctor hasn’t set their password yet, you can re-send the invitation at
any time to give them a fresh link.

The doctor profile
Each doctor’s profile keeps:

Identity and contact details
Registration and credentials (registration number, degree, specialisation,
year of registration)
Hospital and department
Employment type
Consultation fee
A digital signature image used on printed prescriptions
Account status — active, inactive or suspended — and whether
their email is verified and password is set

Editing and removing doctors

Edit any detail except the email address (which is fixed once the
doctor is created). If you move a doctor to a different hospital, their
department is checked against the new hospital.
Deactivate or remove a doctor who no longer practises at your
organisation.

What doctors do next
Once invited, a doctor manages their own working hours and time-off, sees
their appointment queue, and records consultations. See:

My Appointments & Availability
Consultations
Prescriptions

45.title: Managing Doctors > Editing and removing doctors

46.title: Managing Doctors > What doctors do next

47.title: Hospitals & Departments
47.text:
Hospitals & Departments
This is where your organisation’s structure is built. A hospital holds its
departments and its physical layout — floors, rooms and beds — which the
rest of MedOps uses for scheduling, admissions and billing.


  

Who manages this
Setting up hospitals, departments and the ward layout is done by the
Tenant Admin. Other roles can view this information, and nurses and
operations staff can update bed status.

Hospitals
A hospital record represents one facility in your organisation. When you
create or edit a hospital, you can set:

Hospital name and an optional branch name
Location — country, state, city and full address
Hospital type — Clinic, Multi-specialty, or Diagnostic
Departments offered — such as OPD, IPD, ICU, Emergency, Pharmacy,
Laboratory and Radiology
Compliance details — GST identification number, clinical
establishment licence, ABHA facility ID, and a drug licence number (needed
when the hospital runs a pharmacy)

Each hospital is Active or Inactive. New hospitals start out active.
You can: create a hospital, edit its details, view it, and deactivate
it when it’s no longer in use.
Departments
Departments are the clinical divisions inside a hospital — for example
Cardiology, Orthopaedics, or the ICU. They can be added, renamed and removed
at any time as your hospital changes.

A department has a name and belongs to one hospital.
Department names must be unique within a hospital — you can’t have two
departments with the same name in the same hospital.

You can: list a hospital’s departments, add a department, rename one,
and remove one you no longer need.
The ward layout: floors, rooms & beds
To admit patients, you first describe the physical space. MedOps uses a
simple hierarchy:


  
  
  

Floor → Room → Bed
A floor contains rooms, and a room contains beds. A patient
is admitted to a specific bed.

Floors
A floor is a level of the building (for example, “Ground Floor” or
“3rd Floor”). Each floor has a number and name, and can optionally be linked
to a department (such as an ICU floor).
Rooms
A room sits on a floor and is where patients stay. Each room has:

A room number (unique on its floor) and an optional name
A room type — General Ward, Semi-Private, Twin Sharing, Private
Deluxe, Suite, ICU, CCU, or Emergency Room
Optional amenities (such as AC, TV, oxygen port or Wi-Fi) for the
patient’s information

Beds
A bed is the individual place a patient is admitted to. Each bed has a
number and an automatically generated bed code that identifies it
exactly (its hospital, floor, room and bed). Every bed has a status:



Bed status
Meaning




Vacant
Ready for the next patient


Occupied
A patient is currently admitted here


Under maintenance
Temporarily out of use


Reserved for cleaning
Being prepared after a discharge or transfer





  
  

Beds protect themselves
A bed that’s occupied can’t be edited or deleted, and its status can’t be
changed manually — it frees up automatically when the patient is discharged
or transferred.

Room pricing (tariffs)
Each room type can have a daily tariff so inpatient charges are
consistent. A tariff is made up of:

Base room rent per day
Nursing charge per day
Doctor (RMO) visit fee per day
Amenity surcharge per day

MedOps adds these up into a total daily charge for the room type, which
applies when a patient is admitted to a room of that type.
Related pages

Admitted Patients (IPD) — use beds to admit
patients
An Inpatient Admission — the full
walkthrough

51.title: Hospitals & Departments > The ward layout: floors, rooms & beds

57.title: Medicines Catalogue
57.text:
Medicines Catalogue
The medicines catalogue is the single, shared list of medicines used
everywhere in MedOps — when doctors prescribe and when the pharmacy manages
stock and billing. Keeping one catalogue means everyone refers to the same
medicines.


  

Who manages this
Everyone can browse and search the catalogue. The Tenant Admin and
doctors can add medicines; only the Tenant Admin can edit or remove
them.

What a medicine record holds

Name
Strength — for example, “500 mg” or “5 mg/5 ml”
Form — for example, tablet, syrup or injection
Manufacturer
Composition — the active ingredients
Pack size — for example, “strip of 10 tablets”
Type — the medicine category
A unique code assigned automatically



  
  
  

Name + strength is unique
Each medicine is unique by its name and strength together. The same
medicine at a different strength is a separate entry, so the right one is
always picked.

Finding medicines
Search the catalogue by name, strength, form, manufacturer or composition,
and page through the results. This is the same search doctors use while
prescribing and the pharmacy uses while billing.
Adding and maintaining medicines

Add — the Tenant Admin can add medicines, and doctors can add one on
the spot while writing a prescription, so a missing medicine never holds
up a consultation.
Edit — the Tenant Admin can update a medicine’s details.
Remove — the Tenant Admin can remove a medicine that’s no longer used.



  
  

History is protected
A medicine that’s already been used on a prescription can’t be removed, so
past prescriptions always stay complete and accurate.

59.title: Medicines Catalogue > What a medicine record holds

61.title: Medicines Catalogue > Adding and maintaining medicines

62.title: My Appointments & Availability
62.text:
My Appointments & Availability
This is a doctor’s personal space. Here you see the appointments booked for
you and control exactly when the front desk can book you.


  
  
  
  
  

Who uses this
This area is for Doctors. The hours and time-off you set here directly
control what the front desk can book.

Your appointment queue
Your queue lists the appointments booked for you. Open any appointment to see
the patient, the reason for the visit and the scheduled time, then start a
consultation when you’re ready.
Your weekly working hours
Set the days and times you see patients:

Working days and hours — a start and end time for each day you work
Appointment length — how long each slot is (for example, 15 or 30
minutes), which decides how many slots fit in a day
Active days — turn individual days on or off



  
  
  
  
  

Save the whole week at once
Your weekly hours are saved as a complete picture. If no days are switched
on, you’re shown as unavailable and no appointments can be booked.

Leave (time away)
Block whole days when you’re away — for annual leave, training or anything
else. Each leave entry has a start and end date and an optional note.
Time-off blocks
Block specific times on a particular day — for a meeting, a procedure or a
break — by setting the date and the start and end time, with an optional
note.


  
  
  

Edit before it starts
You can change or remove leave and time-off only before it begins. Once
a leave period or blocked time has started, it’s locked — so add upcoming
absences ahead of time.

How this affects booking
The front desk only sees times when you’re genuinely free. MedOps
automatically keeps bookings out of your non-working hours, your leave, and
your blocked times, and prevents two patients landing in the same slot.
See Appointments for the booking side.

63.title: My Appointments & Availability > My Appointments & Availability

64.title: My Appointments & Availability > Your appointment queue

65.title: My Appointments & Availability > Your weekly working hours

68.title: My Appointments & Availability > How this affects booking

69.title: Operations Team
69.text:
Operations Team
The Operations team is your front desk — the people who register
patients, book appointments, handle admissions and take payments. Operations
staff are added and managed by the Tenant Admin.


  

Who manages this
The Tenant Admin adds, edits and removes operations staff. Each
operations user belongs to one hospital.

Adding an operations user
To add a front-desk team member, you provide:

Name
Email address
Hospital — the hospital they’ll work at

MedOps creates their account and emails them their login details so they can
sign in straight away and set their own password.


  
  

Re-send credentials any time
If a new staff member hasn’t signed in yet, you can re-send their login
details to give them a fresh link.

The operations profile
Each operations user has:

Name, email and contact details
Location (country, state, city)
The hospital they’re assigned to
Account status — active, inactive or suspended — and whether
their email is verified and password is set

Editing and removing staff

Edit a staff member’s name, status, or hospital assignment.
Remove an account when someone leaves.

What operations staff do
Day to day, the operations team uses MedOps to:

Register patients and keep their details current
Book and manage appointments against doctors’
availability
Admit, transfer and discharge inpatients
Record payments and track unpaid bills

See the Operations role guide for a day-in-the-life
overview.

71.title: Operations Team > Adding an operations user

73.title: Operations Team > Editing and removing staff

74.title: Operations Team > What operations staff do

75.title: Patients
75.text:
Patients
Everything in MedOps starts with the patient record. A patient is registered
once, and every appointment, consultation, prescription, admission and
bill links back to that single record.


  

Who manages this
Operations staff and the Tenant Admin register and manage patients.
Doctors see patient information through the appointments and consultations
they’re part of.

Registering a patient
When you register a patient, you capture:

Full name
Gender — male, female or other
Date of birth
Phone number
Email — optional
Address — optional
Aadhaar number — optional
Hospital — where the patient is registered



  
  

A unique MRN, automatically
MedOps gives every patient a unique Medical Record Number (MRN) the
moment they’re registered. You never have to assign one yourself, and it
stays with the patient for life.

Finding patients
You can browse all patients or search to jump straight to the right person.
Search looks across:

Name
MRN
Phone number
Email

You can also filter the list by hospital, and page through large lists.
Viewing a patient
Open a patient to see their full profile along with their history:

Visit timeline — their recent appointments, each showing the visit
type and status, the doctor’s consultation notes, any follow-up date, and
the medicines that were prescribed
Prescription history — every prescription issued to the patient, with
the prescribing doctor, the date, and each medicine’s dosage, frequency,
duration and instructions

This gives anyone caring for the patient the full picture at a glance.
Updating a patient
You can update a patient’s details — name, gender, date of birth, phone,
email, address and Aadhaar number — at any time.


  
  

The hospital stays fixed
A patient’s registered hospital can’t be changed after registration, so
their records stay anchored to where they were first seen.

Related pages

Appointments — book a visit for a patient
An Outpatient Visit — the end-to-end flow

82.title: Pharmacies
82.text:
Pharmacies
A pharmacy is a dispensing unit in your organisation, with its own stock,
prices, tax rates and logins. You can run one pharmacy or several. Pharmacies
are set up by the Tenant Admin.


  

Who manages this
The Tenant Admin creates pharmacies and manages their logins. The
pharmacy team works inside the Pharmacy Portal.

Setting up a pharmacy
When you create a pharmacy, you set:

Name — unique within your organisation (for example, “RK Pharmacy —
Anna Nagar”)
Address — optional
Phone — optional
Tax rates — the CGST and SGST percentages applied to invoices



  
  
  

Tax changes don’t rewrite the past
Each invoice keeps a copy of the tax rates that applied when it was created.
If you update a pharmacy’s rates later, only new invoices use the new rates.

You can edit a pharmacy’s details and view all pharmacies in your
organisation, along with how many logins each one has.
Pharmacy logins
Each pharmacy can have several logins for its team. As the Tenant Admin you
can:

Create a login — MedOps emails the new user a link to set their
password and sign in.
List logins — see each pharmacy’s users and whether they’ve set a
password.
Remove a login — revoke access when someone leaves.
Re-send an invite — give a fresh setup link to someone who hasn’t
signed in yet.



  
  
  

Each login is tied to one pharmacy
A pharmacy login always works within its own pharmacy, so its team only ever
sees that pharmacy’s stock, prices and invoices.

Related pages

Pharmacy Portal — what the pharmacy team does
day to day
Medicines Catalogue — the shared medicine list

87.title: Pharmacy Portal
87.text:
Pharmacy Portal
The Pharmacy Portal is where the pharmacy team works. It brings together
stock, supplier deliveries, prescription dispensing and billing — all for
your own pharmacy.


  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  

Who uses this
The Pharmacy team. Everything you see is specific to the pharmacy you
sign in to.

Stock and prices
See what’s on hand at a glance — each medicine’s quantity and selling price —
and search by name, code or strength.

Set a selling price for any medicine. If it isn’t stocked yet, this
creates the entry ready for its first delivery.
Fill in medicine details like form and strength when they’re missing,
without leaving the portal.

Receiving supplies
When stock arrives from a supplier, record the delivery so your stock stays
accurate. A supply record captures:

Supplier name and an optional reference (such as the supplier’s
invoice or delivery-note number)
An optional note
The medicines received — each with a quantity and selling price, and an
optional purchase cost for your own records

Recording a supply adds the quantities to your stock and can update the
selling price for each medicine.
Dispensing against a prescription
Enter the prescription number. MedOps shows the prescribed medicines
next to your current stock and prices.
Check the warning, if any. If this prescription has already been
billed, MedOps flags it so you don’t bill it twice.
Create the invoice. Prices come from your stock and tax is added
automatically; your stock is reduced as you dispense.
Take payment by cash or bank transfer.

Walk-in sales
You can also sell over the counter to customers who aren’t registered
patients — just record the customer’s name and the medicines, and bill the
same way.
What’s on an invoice

An invoice number assigned automatically
The customer — the patient (with MRN) or the walk-in customer’s name
The prescription reference, when it’s a prescription sale
The medicines, each with quantity, price and line total
The subtotal, the CGST and SGST amounts, and the total
The payment method (cash or bank transfer) and status

You can list and search your invoices, and open any invoice to view or print
it.
Built-in protection


  
  

Accurate stock and pricing, always

Stock never goes negative. If two sales reach for the last units, only
one succeeds — the other is told stock has run out.
Prices are set by your pharmacy, not entered at the till, so billing
stays consistent.
Tax is recorded per invoice, so later rate changes never alter past
invoices.


Reference
While receiving supplies or billing, you can browse the shared
Medicines Catalogue for medicine details. For the full
flow, see Pharmacy Dispensing.

91.title: Pharmacy Portal > Dispensing against a prescription

93.title: Pharmacy Portal > What’s on an invoice

96.title: Prescriptions
96.text:
Prescriptions
A prescription lists the medicines a doctor has prescribed, with clear
instructions for the patient and the pharmacy. Prescriptions are created as
part of completing a consultation, so there’s no
separate step to remember.
What’s on a prescription
Each prescription has its own reference number and one or more medicines.
For every medicine, the prescription records:

Medicine — name and strength
Dosage — how much to take (for example, “1 tablet” or “500 mg”)
Frequency — how often (for example, “twice a day after food”)
Duration — how many days to take it
Quantity — how many to dispense
Instructions — any special guidance (optional)

It can also include the doctor’s clinical notes and a follow-up date.
Printing a prescription
A printed prescription is ready to hand to the patient and includes:

The hospital’s letterhead — name, address and logo
The doctor’s details and signature
The patient’s details (or the beneficiary’s, when the visit was booked
on someone’s behalf)
The medicines with full dosage instructions
The prescription reference number and date



  
  
  
  

Who can view and print
Clinical and front-desk staff can open and print a prescription using its
reference number.

How it reaches the pharmacy
The reference number is the link between the prescription and the pharmacy:
The patient takes the prescription to the pharmacy (or the number is
shared from the consultation).
The pharmacist looks it up by its reference number and sees the
medicines alongside the pharmacy’s own stock and prices.
The pharmacy dispenses and bills the medicines, and stock is reduced
automatically.



  
  
  

Consultation fee vs. medicines
The consultation fee is billed by the hospital when the consultation is
completed. The medicines are billed separately by the pharmacy at the
pharmacy’s prices.

Inpatient prescriptions
For admitted patients, doctors can add medications throughout the stay rather
than a single prescription. See
Admitted Patients (IPD).
Related pages

Pharmacy Portal — dispensing against a
prescription
Pharmacy Dispensing — the full
walkthrough

100.title: Prescriptions > How it reaches the pharmacy

103.title: Your Account & Profile
103.text:
Your Account & Profile
Your profile holds your personal details and sign-in settings. You can view
it any time and update the parts that are yours to manage.
What’s on your profile
Your profile shows:

Name
Email address — this is also how you sign in
Phone number
Location — country, state and city
Role — for example, Doctor or Operations
Email verification status — whether your email has been confirmed
Account status — active, inactive, or suspended
Inactivity sign-out time — how long until you’re signed out when idle

Editing your details
You can update the following yourself:

Full name
Phone number
Country, state and city



  
  

Some details are managed for you
Your email address, role, organisation, and hospital
assignment are set by your administrator and can’t be changed from your
profile. If any of these are wrong, ask your Tenant Admin to update them.

Changing your password
If you know your current password, you can change it at any time:
Open the change-password screen from your profile.
Enter your current password.
Enter a new password. It must be at least 8 characters with an
uppercase letter, a number, and a special character — and it must be
different from your current one.
Save. For your security, changing your password signs you out on
every device, so sign in again with the new password.



  
  
  

Signed in with a one-time code?
If you usually sign in with an emailed code rather than a password, use
Forgot password on the sign-in page to set a password instead — the
change-password screen needs an existing password to work.

Keeping your account secure

Use a password you don’t use anywhere else.
Always Sign out on shared or public computers.
If you think someone else knows your password, change it right away —
this immediately signs out every device.

104.title: Your Account & Profile > Your Account & Profile

105.title: Your Account & Profile > What’s on your profile

106.title: Your Account & Profile > Editing your details

107.title: Your Account & Profile > Changing your password

108.title: Your Account & Profile > Keeping your account secure

109.title: Signing In & The Basics
109.text:
Signing In & The Basics
This page covers everything you need to get into MedOps and find your way
around for the first time.
How you get an account
There are two ways an account is created:

Your administrator invites you. Most people (doctors, nurses, staff,
operations and pharmacy users) are added by their organisation’s Tenant
Admin and receive an email invitation to set up their password.
You sign up to start a new organisation. If you’re setting up MedOps
for your hospital group for the first time, you sign up as the Tenant
Admin, which also creates your organisation.



  
  
  

Most users don’t sign up
If your hospital already uses MedOps, you don’t sign up — you wait for an
invitation email and set your password from the link inside it.

Setting up your password for the first time
When your administrator adds you, MedOps emails you a secure setup link (or
a one-time code, depending on your role).
Open the invitation email
Look for the email from MedOps with your login details and a setup link.
Open the setup link
The link takes you to a page where you choose your own password. Setup
links are time-limited for security, so use yours soon after it arrives.
Choose a strong password
Your password must be at least 8 characters and include at least one
uppercase letter, one number, and one special character
(such as ! @ # $).
Sign in
Once your password is set, you’re signed in and ready to start.



  
  
  

Got a temporary password instead?
Some invitations include a temporary password. Sign in with it, and MedOps
will immediately ask you to set a new password of your own before you
continue.

Signing in
Go to the sign-in page and enter your email address.
Enter your password. If you haven’t set one yet, MedOps will email
you a one-time code so you can set it up.
You’re in. You’ll land on the home screen for your role.



  
  
  
  

Shared computers are fine
MedOps keeps each role’s sign-in separate. On a shared front-desk
computer, this means different roles can be signed in side by side without
interfering with one another.

Forgot your password?
Choose “Forgot password” on the sign-in page and enter your email.
Check your email. MedOps sends a one-time code and a reset link.
Set a new password using the code or the link. The same password
rules apply.
Sign in again. For your security, resetting your password signs you
out everywhere, so sign in again on each device.



  

Privacy first
For security, the reset screen always says the email was sent — it won’t
reveal whether an address is registered. If you don’t receive anything,
check the address and your spam folder, or ask your administrator.

Sessions and staying signed in
MedOps signs you out automatically after a period of inactivity, which
protects patient information on unattended screens.

The default is 15 minutes of inactivity.
Your organisation’s Tenant Admin can change this (anywhere from 1 minute
up to 24 hours).
The timer resets while you’re working — you only get signed out if
you step away and stop using the system.

Signing out
Use Sign out whenever you finish, especially on shared computers.
Signing out ends your session immediately.
What’s next

Your Account & Profile — update
your details and change your password.
Roles at a Glance — find the guide for
your job.

110.title: Signing In & The Basics > Signing In & The Basics

118.title: MedOps HMS — User Guide
118.text:
Welcome to MedOps HMS
MedOps HMS is a hospital management platform that keeps your whole
hospital — front desk, doctors, wards, pharmacy and billing — working
from one shared patient record. Register a patient once, and every
appointment, consultation, prescription, admission and bill flows from
that single record.
This guide explains how to use MedOps in everyday work. Pick your role,
follow a step-by-step guide, or browse the feature pages.


  
  
  
  

New here?
Start with Signing In & The Basics, then jump to the
guide for your role.

Explore the platform




  
  

Patients & Appointments
Register patients, book conflict-checked appointments, and keep a full
visit history. Open →





  
  
  
  

Consultations & Prescriptions
Record a consultation and have the prescription and consultation bill
created in one step. Open →







  
  
  
  

Inpatient Care (IPD)
Admit patients to beds, record ward rounds, transfer and discharge.
Open →





  
  

Pharmacy
Dispense against prescriptions, manage stock, and bill with tax.
Open →







  
  
  

Billing & Payments
Collect payments, track unpaid bills, and keep clean records.
Open →





  

Setup & Administration
Create hospitals, departments, beds, staff and preferences.
Open →



Find your way around

Just getting started? → Getting Started
Want the big picture? → Overview and Key Features
Looking for your daily tasks? → Role Guides
Need a walkthrough? → Step-by-Step Guides
Stuck on a term? → Glossary and FAQ

Get Started

119.title: MedOps HMS — User Guide > Welcome to MedOps HMS

120.title: MedOps HMS — User Guide > Explore the platform

121.title: MedOps HMS — User Guide > Find your way around

122.title: Overview
122.text:
Overview
MedOps HMS is a complete hospital management platform. It connects the
people and tasks involved in running a hospital — registering patients,
booking appointments, seeing doctors, prescribing and dispensing
medicines, admitting and discharging inpatients, and collecting payments —
around a single, shared patient record.
One record, one workflow
Most hospital software keeps each department on its own island. MedOps is
different: a patient is registered once, and everything that happens
afterwards is linked back to that one record.

The front desk registers a patient and books an appointment.
The doctor opens that appointment, records the consultation, and writes
a prescription.
The system creates the consultation bill automatically.
The pharmacy pulls up the same prescription to dispense and bill the
medicines.
If the patient is admitted, the ward records rounds and the stay is
tracked to discharge.

Everyone sees a consistent picture, and nothing has to be re-typed from
one screen to the next.
How MedOps is organised


  
  
  
  
  

Organisation, hospitals, and people
MedOps is built for healthcare organisations. Your organisation can
run one hospital or several. All of your hospitals, staff, patients and
records live inside your organisation and are kept completely separate
from every other organisation on the platform.


Your organisation is the top level — created when your administrator
first signs up.
Hospitals belong to your organisation. Each hospital has its own
departments, doctors, beds, patients and pharmacy.
People (your staff) are given a role that decides what they can see
and do, and are usually tied to a specific hospital.

Who uses MedOps
MedOps supports six roles, each tailored to a job in the hospital:



Role
Who they are




Tenant Admin
Owns the organisation; sets up hospitals, staff and preferences


Doctor
Sees patients, records consultations, writes prescriptions, does ward rounds


Nurse
Supports bedside and ward care


Staff
General hospital staff


Operations
Front desk — registration, appointments, admissions, payments


Pharmacy
Runs the pharmacy: stock, dispensing and billing



See Roles at a Glance for what each role
can do, or jump straight to your Role Guide.
What you can do




  
  

Run the front desk
Register patients, book and reschedule appointments, admit and
discharge inpatients, and take payments.





  
  
  
  
  

Deliver care
Record consultations and vitals, prescribe medicines, set follow-ups,
and round on admitted patients.







  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  

Run the pharmacy
Receive stock, set prices, dispense against prescriptions, and bill
walk-in customers — all with tax handled for you.





  
  
  

Stay in control
Manage hospitals, departments, beds and staff, and review a complete
activity log of who did what and when.



Where to go next

Key Features — a tour of everything MedOps does
Signing In & The Basics — get into the system
Step-by-Step Guides — follow a complete workflow

129.title: Key Features
129.text:
Key Features
Here is the full picture of what MedOps HMS offers. Every feature shares
the same patient record and the same set of roles, so the pieces fit
together end to end.
Front desk & patients




  
  

Patient registration
Register a patient once with their personal and contact details. Each
patient gets a unique Medical Record Number (MRN) automatically.
Learn more →





  
  
  
  
  

Appointments
Book appointments against a doctor’s real availability. MedOps blocks
double-bookings and bookings during a doctor’s leave.
Learn more →



Clinical care




  
  
  
  

Consultations
Doctors record notes and follow-up dates. Completing a consultation
creates the prescription and the consultation bill in a single step.
Learn more →





  
  
  
  

Prescriptions
Clear, printable prescriptions with medicines, dosage, frequency and
duration — ready for the patient and the pharmacy.
Learn more →







  
  
  
  

Admitted patients (IPD)
Admit patients to specific beds, record ward rounds and vitals, add
inpatient medications, transfer beds, and discharge.
Learn more →





  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  

My appointments & availability
Doctors see their own appointment queue and set their weekly working
hours, leave and time-off.
Learn more →



Pharmacy




  
  

Medicines catalogue
A shared, searchable list of medicines used across prescriptions and
pharmacy stock.
Learn more →





  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  

Pharmacy portal
Receive stock, set selling prices, dispense against prescriptions,
handle walk-in sales, and bill with tax calculated for you.
Learn more →



Money & oversight




  
  
  

Billing & payments
Consultation and pharmacy bills are generated for you. Record
payments, track what’s unpaid, and cancel mistakes.
Learn more →





  
  
  

Activity log
A complete, searchable record of who did what and when across the
organisation.
Learn more →



Setup & everyday tools




  
  
  
  
  

Hospitals & departments
Set up each hospital and its departments, then build out floors, rooms
and beds.
Learn more →





  
  
  
  

Staff management
Add and manage doctors and operations (front-desk) staff, and invite
them by email.
Learn more →







  
  
  
  

Dashboard
A role-aware home screen that puts your day’s work and key numbers
within easy reach.
Learn more →





  
  

Global search
Find patients, doctors, appointments, bills and more from one search
box — limited to what your role can see.
Learn more →



Built-in safeguards


  
  

Designed to prevent everyday mistakes

No double-booking — you can’t book a doctor who is busy, on leave, or off-duty.
No lost charges — consultation and pharmacy bills are created automatically.
No stock surprises — dispensing reduces stock and is blocked when stock runs out.
Nothing slips through — every important action is recorded in the activity log.

137.title: Roles at a Glance
137.text:
Roles at a Glance
Every person who uses MedOps signs in with a role. Your role decides
which screens you see and what you’re allowed to do. This keeps the system
simple for each person and keeps sensitive information in the right hands.
The six roles



Role
Best for
What they mainly do




Tenant Admin
Organisation owner / hospital administration
Set up hospitals, departments, beds, staff and preferences; oversee billing and the activity log


Doctor
Treating physicians
See their appointment queue, record consultations, write prescriptions, and round on admitted patients


Nurse
Ward and bedside nursing staff
Support inpatient care and view patient and ward information


Staff
General hospital staff
Day-to-day access to shared information appropriate to their work


Operations
Front desk / reception
Register patients, book appointments, admit and discharge patients, and take payments


Pharmacy
Pharmacy team
Manage stock, dispense against prescriptions, and bill medicines



What this means for you


  
  
  
  
  

Your view is tailored to your role
You’ll only see the menus and actions that apply to your job. If you ever
expect to see something and can’t, it’s usually because it belongs to a
different role — your administrator can confirm what your account can
access.

Read your role guide




  

Tenant Admin
Setup and oversight of the whole organisation.
Open guide →





  
  
  
  
  

Doctor
Consultations, prescriptions and ward rounds.
Open guide →







  
  

Nurse
Supporting bedside and ward care.
Open guide →





  
  

Staff
General hospital staff access.
Open guide →







  
  

Operations (Front Desk)
Registration, scheduling, admissions and payments.
Open guide →





  
  

Pharmacy
Stock, dispensing and pharmacy billing.
Open guide →

138.title: Roles at a Glance > Roles at a Glance

139.title: Roles at a Glance > The six roles

140.title: Roles at a Glance > What this means for you

141.title: Roles at a Glance > Read your role guide

142.title: FAQ
142.text:
Frequently Asked Questions
Getting in

        
            I didn’t get my invitation email
            
        
        
Check your spam folder first. If it’s still missing, ask your Tenant Admin to
re-send your invitation — they can generate a fresh setup link for you at any
time before you’ve set a password.


        
            I forgot my password
            
        
        
Use Forgot password on the sign-in page. MedOps emails you a one-time
code and a reset link. After resetting, you’ll need to sign in again on each
device. See Signing In & The Basics.


        
            Why was I signed out?
            
        
        
MedOps signs you out automatically after a period of inactivity to protect
patient information. The timer resets while you’re working. Your Tenant Admin
sets how long the system waits (15 minutes by default).


        
            Can two people use the same front-desk computer?
            
        
        
Yes. MedOps keeps each role’s sign-in separate, so different roles can be
signed in on a shared computer without clashing.

Patients & appointments

        
            How does a patient get their MRN?
            
        
        
It’s assigned automatically the moment you register the patient — you never
create one yourself, and it never changes.


        
            Why can’t I book a doctor at a certain time?
            
        
        
The time is likely outside the doctor’s working hours, during their leave or a
blocked time, or already booked. MedOps shows a doctor’s availability and
hides times that can’t be used, so only valid slots can be chosen.


        
            Can I change which hospital a patient belongs to?
            
        
        
No. A patient’s registered hospital is fixed so their records stay anchored to
where they were first seen. Everything else about the patient can be edited.

Care & billing

        
            Do I need to create the bill after a consultation?
            
        
        
No. Completing a consultation automatically issues the prescription, creates
the consultation bill, and marks the appointment completed. The front desk
just records the payment.


        
            How does the pharmacy get the prescription?
            
        
        
Through the prescription’s reference number. The pharmacist enters it to pull
up the medicines, then dispenses and bills them at the pharmacy’s prices. See
Pharmacy Portal.


        
            A medicine I need isn’t in the list
            
        
        
Doctors can add a new medicine to the catalogue while writing a prescription,
so it never holds up the visit. Tenant Admins can also add, edit and remove
medicines. See Medicines Catalogue.


        
            What happens to a bed after a patient leaves?
            
        
        
It’s flagged reserved for cleaning automatically after a discharge or
transfer, so the front desk only assigns beds that are ready.

Roles & access

        
            Why can’t I see a feature I expected?
            
        
        
MedOps shows only what your role needs. If you think you should have access to
something, ask your Tenant Admin to check your account. See
Roles at a Glance.


        
            Who can see the activity log?
            
        
        
The activity log is available to the Tenant Admin, giving a single view of
who did what and when across the organisation. See
Activity Log.


        
            Can I edit my leave after it starts?
            
        
        
No — leave and time-off can be changed or removed only before they begin, so
add upcoming absences ahead of time. See
My Appointments & Availability.

148.title: Glossary
148.text:
Glossary
Quick definitions for the terms you’ll come across in MedOps.
People & places

Organisation — your healthcare group. It owns one or more hospitals,
and all your data is kept separate from every other organisation.
Hospital — a single facility in your organisation, with its own
departments, staff, beds and pharmacy.
Department — a clinical division within a hospital, such as Cardiology
or the ICU.
Role — what a person is allowed to do in MedOps: Tenant Admin, Doctor,
Nurse, Staff, Operations or Pharmacy.

Patients & visits

MRN (Medical Record Number) — a unique number assigned to each patient
automatically at registration; it stays with them for life.
Beneficiary — the person actually seen when an outpatient visit is
booked on someone else’s behalf (for example, a family member).
OP (Outpatient) / OPD — a visit where the patient is seen and goes home
the same day.
IP (Inpatient) / IPD — care for a patient admitted to a bed for a stay.
Follow-up — a return visit after an earlier consultation.
No-show — an appointment where the patient didn’t arrive.

Clinical

Consultation — the record of a doctor seeing a patient, including notes
and any prescription.
Prescription — the list of medicines a doctor prescribes, with dosage,
frequency and duration.
Ward round — a doctor’s recorded review of an admitted patient.
Vitals — measurements such as blood pressure, temperature, pulse,
oxygen level (SpO₂) and breathing rate.

Wards & beds

Floor → Room → Bed — the layout of a hospital. A patient is admitted to
a specific bed.
Room type — the category of a room (General Ward, Semi-Private, Twin
Sharing, Private Deluxe, Suite, ICU, CCU or Emergency Room).
Bed code — a unique label that identifies a bed by its hospital, floor,
room and bed number.
Tariff — the daily charge for a room type, made up of room rent,
nursing, doctor visit and amenity charges.
Admission / Discharge / Transfer — admitting a patient to a bed,
releasing them at the end of the stay, and moving them between beds.

Money

Bill — a charge raised by the hospital, such as a consultation fee.
Invoice — a pharmacy’s bill for dispensed medicines.
Advance deposit — money collected upfront at admission.
Payment type — how an inpatient stay is paid for: self-pay, cashless or
corporate.
CGST / SGST — the central and state tax components applied to pharmacy
invoices.
GSTIN — a hospital’s tax identification number.

Other

Activity log — the record of who did what and when across the
organisation.
Inactivity sign-out — being signed out automatically after a period of
not using the system.

156.title: Doctor
156.text:
Doctor
As a Doctor you focus on patient care. MedOps gives you your own
appointment queue, a place to record each visit, and the tools to
prescribe and follow up — all linked to the patient’s record.
Your day in MedOps




  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  

My appointments
See the appointments booked for you and open each one to start a
consultation.
Open →





  
  
  
  
  

My availability
Set your weekly working hours, slot length, leave and time-off so the
front desk only books you when you’re free.
Open →







  
  
  
  

Consultations
Record notes and a follow-up date. Completing the consultation creates
the prescription and the consultation bill automatically.
Open →





  
  
  
  

Prescriptions
Add medicines with dosage, frequency and duration. Prescriptions are
printable and flow straight to the pharmacy.
Open →



Seeing an outpatient
Open the appointment from your queue.
Start the consultation and record your clinical notes.
Add a prescription if needed — search the medicines list and set the
dosage, frequency and duration for each medicine.
Set a follow-up date if the patient should return.
Complete the consultation. MedOps issues the prescription, creates
the consultation bill, and marks the appointment as completed — all in
one step.

For the full walkthrough, see
An Outpatient Visit.
Caring for admitted patients
When your patients are admitted, you can:

Record ward rounds with notes, vitals (blood pressure, temperature,
pulse, oxygen level, breathing rate), your assessment, and the plan.
Add inpatient medications as the treatment changes during the stay.

See Admitted Patients (IPD) for details.
Setting your availability
Your availability drives what the front desk can book:

Weekly hours — set start and end times for each working day, plus how
long each appointment slot is.
Leave — block whole days when you’re away.
Time-off blocks — block specific times on a given day (for example, a
meeting or a procedure).



  
  
  

Plan ahead
You can edit or remove leave and time-off only before it starts, so add
upcoming absences early. The front desk can’t book you during your leave or
blocked times.

Good to know

You see your own appointments; the front desk manages booking and
rescheduling.
You can add a new medicine to the shared catalogue on the spot while
prescribing, so a missing medicine never holds you up.

163.title: Nurse
163.text:
Nurse
As a Nurse you support care at the bedside and on the ward. MedOps gives
you the patient and ward information you need, and lets you keep beds ready
for the next admission.
What you can do




  
  
  
  

Follow admitted patients
See admitted patients, their ward rounds and inpatient medications, so
you always have the latest clinical picture.
Open →





  
  
  
  

Keep beds ready
Mark beds as needing cleaning or under maintenance so the front desk
only assigns beds that are truly available.
Open →







  
  

Look up medicines
Browse the shared medicines catalogue for names, strengths and forms.
Open →





  
  
  
  
  

See your hospital
View your hospital and its wards for context while you work.
Open →



Keeping beds ready
When a patient is discharged or transferred, their bed is automatically
flagged for cleaning. You can update a bed’s status to show whether it is:

Vacant — ready for the next patient
Reserved for cleaning — being prepared
Under maintenance — temporarily out of use



  

Occupied beds are protected
You can’t change the status of a bed that has a patient in it. The bed
frees up automatically when the patient is discharged or transferred.

Good to know

Recording ward rounds and prescribing inpatient medicines is done by
doctors; you can view everything they record.
The exact screens you see depend on how your organisation has set up your
account — ask your Tenant Admin if you need access to something.

168.title: Operations (Front Desk)
168.text:
Operations (Front Desk)
As an Operations user you run the front desk. You’re the first point of
contact for patients: registering them, booking their appointments,
admitting and discharging inpatients, and taking payments.
Your toolkit




  
  

Register patients
Add new patients with their details. Each gets a unique Medical Record
Number automatically.
Open →





  
  
  
  
  

Book appointments
Schedule visits against a doctor’s real availability — MedOps prevents
double-booking and bookings during leave.
Open →







  
  
  
  

Admit & discharge
Admit patients to beds, transfer them between beds, and process
discharges.
Open →





  
  
  

Take payments
Record payments on consultation bills, track what’s unpaid, and cancel
bills created in error.
Open →



Booking an appointment
Find or register the patient. Search by name, MRN, phone or email;
if they’re new, register them first.
Pick the doctor and time. MedOps shows the doctor’s available slots
and hides times that are already booked or blocked.
Add the reason for the visit (and any beneficiary details for an
outpatient visit booked on someone’s behalf).
Confirm. The appointment appears in the doctor’s queue.



  
  

MedOps catches conflicts for you
You can’t book a doctor in the past, outside their working hours, during
their leave, or on a slot that’s already taken — so clashes are avoided
before they happen.

Admitting a patient
Choose the patient and an available bed.
Select the admitting doctor and record the reason for admission.
Capture payment details — any advance deposit, the payment type
(self-pay, cashless or corporate), and insurance information if relevant.
Confirm. The bed is marked occupied and the patient is ready for
ward rounds and inpatient medicines.

For the full walkthrough, see
An Inpatient Admission.
Everyday helpers

Global search finds patients, appointments and bills in one place.
Reschedule or cancel appointments when plans change.
Mark no-shows so doctors’ queues stay accurate.

174.title: Pharmacy
174.text:
Pharmacy
As a Pharmacy user you work in the Pharmacy Portal — your dedicated
space for stock, dispensing and billing. You always work within your own
pharmacy, so everything you see is specific to where you sign in.
Your workspace




  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  

Stock & prices
See what’s on hand, set selling prices, and keep medicine details up to
date.
Open →





  
  
  
  
  
Receive supplies
Record deliveries from suppliers to top up your stock.
Open →







  
  
  
  

Dispense prescriptions
Look up a prescription by its number, dispense the medicines, and bill
the patient.
Open →





  
  
  

Walk-in sales
Sell over the counter to customers who aren’t registered patients.
Open →



Dispensing against a prescription
Enter the prescription number (the patient brings it, or it’s shared
from the consultation).
Review the medicines. MedOps shows the prescribed items alongside
your current stock and prices, and warns you if this prescription has
already been billed.
Create the invoice. Prices come from your stock and tax is added
automatically. Your stock goes down as you dispense.
Take payment by cash or bank transfer and hand over the medicines.

For the full walkthrough, see
Pharmacy Dispensing.
Keeping stock accurate

Receiving supplies increases your stock and can update the selling
price for each medicine.
Selling and dispensing decrease your stock automatically.
MedOps won’t let you sell more than you have — if two sales race for
the last units, only one goes through.



  
  
  

Tax is handled for you
Your pharmacy’s tax rates are applied to each invoice automatically, and
each invoice keeps a copy of the rates used at the time — so later changes
never rewrite past invoices.

Good to know

You can browse the shared medicines catalogue for reference while
receiving stock or billing.
Pharmacy records (stock, supplies and invoices) are private to your
pharmacy.

180.title: Staff
180.text:
Staff
The Staff role is for general hospital employees who need day-to-day
access to shared information without front-desk or clinical
responsibilities.
What you can do




  
  
  
  

View admitted patients
See current admissions, ward rounds and inpatient medications for the
patients you support.
Open →





  
  

Browse medicines
Look up medicines in the shared catalogue by name, strength or form.
Open →





  
  
  

Access is tailored to your work
The Staff role is intentionally flexible. What you can see and do depends
on how your organisation has set up your account. If you need access to a
specific area, ask your Tenant Admin.

Who does what
If your job involves any of the following, your administrator may give you a
more specific role instead:

Registering patients and booking appointments → Operations
Recording consultations and prescriptions → Doctor
Dispensing and pharmacy billing → Pharmacy

See Roles at a Glance to compare.

184.title: Tenant Admin
184.text:
Tenant Admin
As a Tenant Admin you own the organisation. You set MedOps up, decide
how it works for everyone else, and keep an eye on the whole operation.


  

Your reach
You can manage everything across your organisation and all of its
hospitals — the structure, the people, the catalogue and the preferences.
Day-to-day clinical and front-desk work is usually done by doctors and
operations staff, but you can always see what’s happening.

Setting up the organisation
Your first job is to build out the structure. A typical order is:
Create your hospitals
Add each hospital with its name, address, licences and departments.
Hospitals & Departments →
Add departments
Create the clinical departments inside each hospital.
Build your wards
Add floors, rooms and beds, and set room pricing (tariffs), so patients
can be admitted later.
Add your people
Invite doctors and operations (front-desk) staff by email.
Managing Doctors → ·
Operations Team →
Set up the pharmacy
Create one or more pharmacies and add pharmacy logins.
Pharmacies →
Tune your preferences
Set branding, document code prefixes, tax details and inpatient billing
rules.

What you manage




  
  
  
  
  

Hospitals & departments
Create, edit and deactivate hospitals; add and rename departments.
Open →





  
  
  
  

Wards, rooms & beds
Lay out floors, rooms and beds, and set daily room tariffs.
Open →







  
  
  
  

Doctors & operations staff
Invite, edit, deactivate and re-invite staff.
Doctors → · Operations →





  
  

Medicines catalogue
Add, edit and remove the medicines used across the organisation.
Open →







  
  
  

Pharmacies & logins
Create pharmacies, set their tax rates, and manage who can sign in.
Open →





  
  
  

Billing oversight
Review bills across the organisation and their payment status.
Open →



Organisation preferences
You control settings that affect everyone:

Branding & identity — each hospital’s display name, address and logo.
Document code prefix — a short prefix (for example, RK) that seeds
the reference codes on prescriptions, invoices and other documents.
Changing it only affects new documents.
Tax details — GST identification for hospitals, and tax rates for
each pharmacy.
Inpatient billing rules — how bed-day charges are counted (by
calendar day or rolling 24 hours) and the grace period before another
day is charged.
Inactivity sign-out time — how long before idle users are signed
out, from 1 minute up to 24 hours (the default is 15 minutes).

Keeping oversight

Activity log — see who did what and when across the organisation,
with search and date filters. Open →
Global search — quickly find patients, doctors, hospitals,
departments, appointments, bills and medicines.

What’s next

New to setup? Follow the First-Time Setup guide.
Want the full feature list? See Key Features.

186.title: Tenant Admin > Setting up the organisation

191.title: First-Time Setup
191.text:
First-Time Setup
This guide walks a Tenant Admin through setting up MedOps from scratch.
Work through it in order — each step builds on the one before.


  

Who this is for
You’ll need the Tenant Admin role. Your organisation was created when you
first signed up; now you’ll fill it with hospitals, wards, people and
preferences.

Create your first hospital
Add the hospital’s name, location, type, the departments it offers, and
its compliance details (licences and tax identification).
Hospitals & Departments →
Add departments
Create the clinical departments inside the hospital (Cardiology, ICU, OPD,
and so on).
Build the ward layout
Add floors, then rooms (with their room type), then beds. This is the
capacity you’ll admit inpatients into.
Set room pricing
Give each room type a daily tariff (base rent, nursing, doctor visit and
amenity charges) so inpatient charges are consistent.
Invite your doctors
Add each doctor with their credentials, department and consultation fee.
They’ll get an email to set their password.
Managing Doctors →
Invite your front desk
Add your operations (front-desk) staff and assign them to a hospital.
Operations Team →
Set up the pharmacy
Create one or more pharmacies with their tax rates, then add pharmacy
logins for the team.
Pharmacies →
Stock the medicines catalogue
Add the medicines your hospital uses (doctors can also add medicines while
prescribing).
Medicines Catalogue →
Set your preferences
Choose your branding and logo, your document code prefix, your inpatient
billing rules, and the inactivity sign-out time.
Tenant Admin guide →



  

You’re ready to go live
Once doctors have set their availability and the front desk is trained,
you’re ready to register patients and book the first appointments.

What’s next

An Outpatient Visit
An Inpatient Admission
Pharmacy Dispensing

194.title: An Inpatient Admission
194.text:
An Inpatient Admission
This guide follows an inpatient (IP) stay from admission to discharge, and
who does what along the way.


  
  
  
  

Who’s involved
Operations (front desk), the Doctor, and Nurses. Make sure your
ward layout — floors, rooms and beds — is set up first.
Set up beds →

The journey
Find or register the patient (Operations)
Search for the patient, or register them if they’re new.
Patients →
Admit to a bed (Operations)
Choose a vacant bed and the admitting doctor, record the reason for
admission, and capture any advance deposit, payment type and insurance
details. The bed is marked occupied.
Admitted Patients →
Round on the patient (Doctor)
Record each ward round with notes, vitals, your assessment and the plan.
The whole care team can see the history.
Manage medications (Doctor)
Add and adjust inpatient medicines as treatment changes during the stay.
Transfer if needed (Operations)
If the patient needs a different bed (for example, the ICU), transfer them
to another vacant bed. The same admission continues with all its history.
Discharge (Operations)
When the patient is ready to leave, process the discharge. MedOps records
the discharge date and frees the bed for cleaning.
Settle charges (Operations)
The room’s daily tariff and the advance deposit inform the final
settlement; any dispensed medicines are billed by the pharmacy.
Billing & Payments →



  
  
  
  

Beds look after themselves
After a discharge or transfer, the bed is automatically flagged for cleaning,
so the front desk only ever assigns beds that are truly ready.

Related guides

An Outpatient Visit
Pharmacy Dispensing

195.title: An Inpatient Admission > An Inpatient Admission

198.title: An Outpatient Visit
198.text:
An Outpatient Visit
This guide follows a typical outpatient (OP) visit from start to finish, and
shows how each role plays its part around the same patient record.


  
  
  
  

Who’s involved
Operations (front desk), the Doctor, and the Pharmacy team.

The journey
Register or find the patient (Operations)
New patient? Register them and MedOps assigns a Medical Record Number.
Returning? Search by name, MRN, phone or email.
Patients →
Book the appointment (Operations)
Choose the doctor and a free slot, set the visit type and reason. MedOps
keeps you within the doctor’s working hours and prevents clashes.
Appointments →
See the patient (Doctor)
Open the appointment from your queue and start the consultation. Record
your notes.
Consultations →
Prescribe (Doctor)
Add the medicines with dosage, frequency and duration, and set a follow-up
date if needed.
Prescriptions →
Complete the consultation (Doctor)
MedOps issues the prescription, creates the consultation bill, and marks
the appointment completed — in one step.
Collect the consultation fee (Operations)
Record the payment against the consultation bill.
Billing & Payments →
Dispense the medicines (Pharmacy)
Look up the prescription by its number, dispense, and bill the medicines.
Pharmacy Portal →



  

One record, start to finish
Because everything is tied to the same patient record, the doctor sees the
booking, the pharmacy sees the prescription, and the patient’s history is
updated automatically — nothing is re-typed.

Related guides

An Inpatient Admission
Pharmacy Dispensing

199.title: An Outpatient Visit > An Outpatient Visit

202.title: Pharmacy Dispensing
202.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing
This guide follows the pharmacy team’s day: keeping stock current, dispensing
against prescriptions, and serving walk-in customers.


  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  

Who’s involved
The Pharmacy team, working in the Pharmacy Portal.

Keep your stock ready
Record supplier deliveries
When stock arrives, record the supplier, the medicines and quantities, and
the selling price for each. Your stock goes up automatically.
Check prices
Make sure each medicine has the right selling price — this is what
billing uses.

Dispense against a prescription
Enter the prescription number
MedOps shows the prescribed medicines next to your stock and prices, and
warns you if the prescription has already been billed.
Create the invoice
Prices come from your stock and tax is added automatically. Your stock is
reduced as you dispense.
Take payment
Record payment by cash or bank transfer, then hand over the medicines and
the invoice.

Serve a walk-in customer
For someone who isn’t a registered patient, skip the prescription number,
enter the customer’s name, choose the medicines from stock, and bill the same
way.


  
  

You’re protected from common errors

You can’t oversell — if stock runs out mid-sale, MedOps stops the sale.
You can’t accidentally bill the same prescription twice — MedOps warns you.
Tax is calculated for you and recorded on each invoice.


Related guides

An Outpatient Visit
Pharmacy Portal

204.title: Pharmacy Dispensing > Keep your stock ready

205.title: Pharmacy Dispensing > Dispense against a prescription

206.title: Pharmacy Dispensing > Serve a walk-in customer
